perturbation.original_text: 4. Upon the termination or expiration of this Agreement for any reason or upon the conclusion of the Services and/or at the request of AMDOCS, the Receiving Party shall: (a) return to AMDOCS any document or other material in tangible form in its possession being part of the AMDOCS Confidential Information; and (b) destroy any document or other material in tangible form that contains the AMDOCS Confidential Information together with confidential and/or proprietary information of a third party, and confirm such destruction in writing to AMDOCS.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Upon the termination or expiration of this Agreement for any reason or upon the conclusion of the Services and/or at the request of AMDOCS, the Receiving Party shall: (a) return to AMDOCS any document or other material in tangible form in its possession being part of the AMDOCS Confidential Information within 90 days; and (b) destroy any document or other material in tangible form that contains the AMDOCS Confidential Information together with confidential and/or proprietary information of a third party within 120 days, and confirm such destruction in writing to AMDOCS.
perturbation.explanation: The modification extends the timeframe for returning and destroying confidential information to 90 and 120 days, respectively. While this might seem reasonable, it could conflict with regulations that require quicker action in protecting sensitive personal information, particularly under data breach notification laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's data breach notification law requires entities to notify affected individuals and state agencies 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay' following the discovery of a breach. While this NDA does not explicitly deal with data breaches, delaying the return or destruction of confidential information (potentially including personally identifiable information) for 90-120 days could be seen as conflicting with the requirement to act expediently in the event of a breach potentially involving that information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York General Business Law § 899-AA (2022) - Notification; Person Without Valid Authorization Has Acquired Private Information. :: 2022 New York Laws :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New York Laws 2024 N.Y. Laws 2023 N.Y. Laws 2022 N.Y. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws GBS - General Business Article 39-F - Notification of Unauthorized Acquisition of Private Information; Data Security Protections 899-AA - Notification; Person Without Valid Authorization Has Acquired Private Information. Universal Citation: NY Gen Bus L § 899-AA (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next § 899-aa. Notification; person without valid authorization has acquired private information. 1. As used in this section, the following terms shall have the following meanings: (a) "Personal information" shall mean any information concerning a natural person which, because of name, number, personal mark, or other identifier, can be used to identify such natural person; (b) "Private information" shall mean either: (i) personal information consisting of any information in combination with any one or more of the following data elements, when either the data element or the combination of personal information plus the data element is not encrypted, or is encrypted with an encryption key that has also been accessed or acquired: (1) social security number; (2) driver's license number or non-driver identification card number; (3) account number, credit or debit card number, in combination with any required security code, access code, password or other information that would permit access to an individual's financial account; (4) account number, credit or debit card number, if circumstances exist wherein such number could be used to access an individual's financial account without additional identifying information, secu
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces specific timeframes (90 and 120 days) for returning and destroying confidential information, which could conflict with the 'most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay' standard in data breach notification laws, as explained by the LLM. While the snippets don't explicitly define 'expedient,' the Perturbation and Law Explanations highlight that delaying action for 90-120 days could violate the spirit and intent of New York's General Business Law Article 39-F, specifically regarding data security protections and notification of unauthorized acquisition of private information (as suggested in Snippet 2, referencing NY Gen Bus L § 899-AA). The original contract requires this to happen upon termination of the agreement or at the request of AMDOCS, which indicates a quicker timeframe. Therefore, the extended timeframes create a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9. This Agreement shall be in full force and effect for a period of seven (7) years commencing on the date first stated above.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. This Agreement shall be in full force and effect for a period of fifteen (15) years commencing on the date first stated above.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the agreement's duration to fifteen years could be problematic if it involves personal data, as data protection laws like those inspired by GDPR often mandate data retention limits based on necessity and purpose limitation. In some US states there are similar regulations in place.
perturbation.contradicted_law: CCPA (California Consumer Privacy Act) - Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.100
perturbation.law_explanation: While the CCPA doesn't specify an exact retention period, it emphasizes that businesses should only retain personal information for as long as is reasonably necessary for the disclosed purposes. A blanket 15-year retention period for all 'AMDOCS Confidential Information,' which could encompass personal data, may violate the CCPA's principles of data minimization and purpose limitation, particularly if the original purpose no longer exists.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the agreement's duration to fifteen years, which may contradict data protection laws inspired by GDPR and similar regulations in some US states, including the CCPA. The Law Explanation states that a blanket 15-year retention period may violate the CCPA's principles of data minimization and purpose limitation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the CCPA gives consumers more control over their personal information and emphasizes that businesses should only retain personal information for as long as is reasonably necessary. Therefore, extending the retention period to 15 years could create a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. The confidentiality obligations of the Receiving Party regarding the AMDOCS Confidential Information shall have not apply to such information which: (a) becomes public domain without fault on the part of the Receiving Party; (b) is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from any source other than AMDOCS, free of any obligation to keep it confidential; (c) is previously known to the Receiving Party without an obligation to keep it confidential, as can be substantiated by written records; (d) is expressly released in writing from such obligations by AMDOCS; or (e) is required to be disclosed pursuant to law, regulation, judicial or administrative order, or request by a governmental or other entity authorized by law to make such request; provided, however, that the Receiving Party first notifies AMDOCS to enable it to seek relief from such requirement, and renders reasonable assistance requested by AMDOCS (at AMDOCS' expense) in connection therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. The confidentiality obligations of the Receiving Party regarding the AMDOCS Confidential Information shall not apply to such information which: (a) becomes public domain without fault on the part of the Receiving Party; (b) is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from any source other than AMDOCS, free of any obligation to keep it confidential; (c) is previously known to the Receiving Party without an obligation to keep it confidential, as can be substantiated by written records; (d) is expressly released in writing from such obligations by AMDOCS; or (e) is required to be disclosed pursuant to law, regulation, judicial or administrative order, or request by a governmental or other entity authorized by law to make such request; provided, however, that the Receiving Party is not required to notify AMDOCS prior to disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for the Receiving Party to notify AMDOCS before disclosing confidential information when required by law creates a conflict. This is because some regulations mandate specific procedures for disclosing information under legal compulsion, including informing the data owner when possible.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA), 45 CFR § 164.512(e)(1)(ii)
perturbation.law_explanation: HIPAA's privacy rule at 45 CFR § 164.512(e)(1)(ii) allows for the disclosure of protected health information (PHI) when required by law, but it also often requires reasonable efforts to notify the individual (or in this case, AMDOCS as the data owner) about the disclosure, unless such notification is prohibited by law. Removing the notification requirement contradicts this aspect of HIPAA, as it prevents AMDOCS from taking steps to protect its information if legally permissible.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for the Receiving Party to notify AMDOCS before disclosing confidential information when required by law. The Law Explanation states that HIPAA's privacy rule often requires reasonable efforts to notify the data owner about the disclosure, unless such notification is prohibited by law. The provided snippet confirms this, stating 45 CFR 164.512 governs uses and disclosures where authorization isn't required. Removing the notification requirement contradicts HIPAA, preventing AMDOCS from protecting its information if legally permissible.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Any use by the Receiving Party of the AMDOCS Confidential Information permitted under this Agreement is conditioned upon the Receiving Party first taking the safeguards and measures required to secure the confidentiality of such Proprietary Information. Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, the Receiving Party shall draw to the attention of its employees who will have access to the AMDOCS Confidential Information, all the obligations concerning the AMDOCS Confidential Information contained in this Agreement, and shall require each and every such employee to sign a written acknowledgment with respect to such obligations substantially in the form of the Annex attached hereto and made a part hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Any use by the Receiving Party of the AMDOCS Confidential Information permitted under this Agreement is conditioned upon the Receiving Party first taking commercially reasonable safeguards and measures to secure the confidentiality of such Proprietary Information. The Receiving Party is not obligated to have employees sign a written acknowledgment regarding these obligations.
perturbation.explanation: The modified clause weakens the security requirements and removes the mandatory employee acknowledgment.  This is a problem. It weakens the protection and goes against data security requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Standards for the Protection of Personal Information of Residents, 201 CMR 17.00
perturbation.law_explanation: 201 CMR 17.03(5) mandates that every person maintaining personal information of a resident of the Commonwealth shall require employees to take steps to maintain the confidentiality of the personal information. Removing the requirement to have a written acknowledgment of the confidentiality agreement is inconsistent with a key element of reasonable security measures under Massachusetts law. Specifically, having each employee 'sign a written acknowledgment' creates a clear and documented commitment to uphold the obligations concerning AMDOCS Confidential Information.

This page, 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth, is offered by Trial Court Law Libraries show more Massachusetts Court System This is a part of the law library : 201 CMR + This is a part of the law library : 201 CMR × Table of Contents 201 CMR 10.00: Weighing, sorting and culling of fish 201 CMR 11.00: New and used motor vehicle arbitration 201 CMR 12.00: Massachusetts do not call registry 201 CMR 14.00: Home Improvement Contractor Arbitration and Guaranty Fund 201 CMR 16.00: Placing, lifting and removal of security freezes 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth 201 CMR 18.00: Registration and enforcement of Home Improvement Contractor program 201 CMR 19.00: Customized wheelchair arbitration Regulation Regulation 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth Date: 11/13/2009 Organization: Office of Consumer Affairs and Business Regulation Regulatory Authority: MGL c. 93H Official Version: Purchase the official print version 201 CMR 17.00 establishes minimum standards to be met by persons who own or license personal information about a resident of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts in connection with the safeguarding of personal information contained in both paper and electronic records, to insure the security and confidentiality of customer information in a manner fully consistent with industry standards; protect against
perturbation.justification: The modified contract eliminates the mandatory employee acknowledgment, which directly contradicts the Law Explanation that cites 201 CMR 17.03(5) as mandating that every person maintaining personal information of a resident of the Commonwealth shall require employees to take steps to maintain the confidentiality of the personal information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms that 201 CMR 17.00 establishes minimum standards to be met by persons who own or license personal information about a resident of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts in connection with the safeguarding of personal information. The removal of the written acknowledgment weakens the security measures, making it likely the modified clause would fail legal scrutiny. This falls under the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction', where a contract sets a requirement that violates state law.
